loci.name: breed-prior-validity-individual-vs-population
loci.one_line: Does breed predict evasion/flight behavior well enough to use as a prior in Nona's intake scoring, or does Morrill 2022 invalidate breed-based priors at the individual level?
loci.rationale: This is the load-bearing design question for Nona's intake form. Morrill 2022 (source: ancestry-inclusive-dog-genomics-challenges-popular-breed-stereotypes-pmc) establishes that agonistic threshold — the exact trait that governs whether a lost dog will flee from rescuers — is the LEAST breed-predictable behavioral factor (breed 'almost uninformative', 9% individual variance). Yet Normando 2024 (source: prevalence-of-some-behavioural-problems-in-two-groups-of-rescue-dogs-galgos-from) shows galgos have significantly higher stranger-fear than other rescue dogs at the population level (p=0.009, n=410). The fight is genuine because the two findings operate at different analytical levels (individual vs. population mean) and the resolution has direct design consequences: Nona should encode breed as a weak population-level prior, not a strong individual classifier. The depth-investigator needs to find: (a) the exact ges/h² values for agonistic threshold from Morrill's supplement, (b) the C-BARQ galgo baseline for stranger-fear as a calibrated probability, and (c) any breed-outcome studies (even informal) that test whether breed assignment improves or degrades recovery probability prediction.

opposing_positions.position: Breed is almost uninformative for agonistic threshold (fear/reactivity) at the individual level. Morrill 2022 (Science, n=18,385) shows breed explains only 9% of individual behavioral variance overall, and agonistic threshold is specifically the least breed-predictable trait. No breed-exclusive behavioral variant exists. The 67.2% within-1-SD overlap across breeds means individual breed assignment yields poor predictive resolution.

Breed-group means on specific fear/evasion traits differ significantly and provide valid weak population-level priors. Normando 2024 (n=410, p=0.009) shows galgo rescue dogs have significantly elevated stranger-fear versus non-galgo rescue dogs. Normando 2025 (n=768) shows sighthound predatory drive differs significantly by breed subtype (p<0.001). Albrecht 1999 shows mixed-breed dogs average 14 miles traveled vs. purebred 2 miles (7x). Population-level priors are valid even when individual prediction is noisy.

suggested_searches: Morrill 2022 agonistic threshold heritability supplement table
C-BARQ galgo stranger fear scores calibrated probability
breed outcome study lost dog recovery rate galgo greyhound sighthound
dog breed behavioral prediction individual vs population prior Bayesian

loci.name: galgo-vs-greyhound-trauma-baseline-transfer
loci.one_line: Can English greyhound chase and freeze-flight research (including OR=8.34 last-known-location fixation) transfer directly to galgo behavioral priors, or does galgo's trauma baseline invalidate the transfer?
loci.rationale: The corpus uses the greyhound chase PMC study (effects-of-lure-type-on-chase-related-behaviour-in-racing-greyhounds-pmc, OR=8.34) as the primary quantitative anchor for predicting where a triggered galgo will stop after a chase. This transfer is load-bearing for Nona's search-radius model in Algarve rabbit habitat. But Normando 2024 (prevalence-of-some-behavioural-problems-in-two-groups-of-rescue-dogs-galgos-from, p=0.009) establishes that rescue galgos have a significantly elevated stranger-fear baseline versus English racing greyhounds — which were professionally handled from birth, not semi-feral working dogs abandoned en masse. The fight is genuine: the chase/predatory-fixation behavior (OR=8.34) likely transfers because it's driven by appetitive predatory mechanics; but the fear/approach-avoidance behavior does not transfer because the trauma history is fundamentally different. The depth-investigator needs to find: (a) any direct galgo field behavior studies in Iberian context, (b) English greyhound vs. galgo comparative fear-of-strangers data, (c) whether the freeze-at-last-known-location behavior is documented in galgos specifically.

opposing_positions.position: Galgos and English greyhounds share the same sighthound predatory motor sequence (chase-fixation on last-known-location, OR=8.34 for last-known-location fixation, appetitive self-reinforcing chase). Greyhound research provides valid priors for galgo chase displacement, prey-triggered flight direction, and post-chase location. Modern galgos are partially interbred with Irish greyhounds. Normando 2025 clusters diverse sighthounds similarly on predatory drive dimensions.

Rescue galgos have a systematically different trauma baseline from racing greyhounds. Normando 2024 (p=0.009, n=410) shows galgo stranger-fear significantly exceeds non-galgo rescue dogs. Galgos 'almost universally' experienced maltreatment; microchips are cut out by hunters; they are abandoned en masse at end of hunting season. Racing greyhounds were professionally socialized from birth. The freeze-to-corner and human-avoidance behavior documented in galgo rescue intake is not documented for English greyhounds at equivalent severity. The trauma-fear layer modifies predatory behavior expression and recovery timeline in galgo-specific ways not captured by greyhound research.

suggested_searches: galgo espanol behavior lost field study Iberian Peninsula
galgo vs greyhound fear strangers comparative study
galgo running behavior prey drive hunt distance
trauma effects predatory motor sequence sighthound
greyhound last known location fixation prey loss field observation

loci.name: dog-phase-thresholds-empirical-anchor
loci.one_line: What are the empirically-calibrated hour and day thresholds for behavioral phase transitions in lost dogs (not cats), and what percentage of dogs are found in each phase?
loci.rationale: The corpus explicitly flags this as its most critical knowledge gap. Coverage-gaps.md (SQ2, SQ5) states: 'No empirical dog-specific threshold data published. Only practitioner heuristics (0-24h/24h-7d). Huang 2018 cat data available as proxy.' The Huang 2018 source analysis (search-methods-used-to-locate-missing-cats-search-methods-locations) provides the only quantitative temporal curve: 34% found alive by day 7, 50% by day 30, 56% by day 61 — but this is for cats, with explicit caveats about transfer. The width corpus has no dog-equivalent temporal study despite citing Lord 2007 repeatedly. Nona's temporal scenario evolution requires specific hour/day thresholds (e.g., 0-4h acute flight, 4-24h transition, 24h+ survival mode) to drive phase-appropriate recommendations. Finding even a single peer-reviewed dog-specific temporal study would change the quantitative scaffolding of the entire engine. The depth-investigator should seek: Lord 2007 full text (paywalled), any shelter-based temporal study of lost dog recovery decay curves, and any phase-threshold framework with citation-backed numbers.
suggested_searches: Lord 2007 JAVMA lost dog recovery time days empirical
lost dog temporal phase behavioral transition hours days empirical study
lost dog survival rate by day week recovery curve
dog missing behavior change over time day 1 vs day 7
shelter lost dog intake timeline stray hold period recovery decay

loci.name: bayesian-sighting-update-lost-dog
loci.one_line: How should a confirmed sighting at time T update the spatial probability distribution for a lost dog at time T+Δ, and what is the correct reliability weighting for eyewitness sightings vs. trail camera evidence?
loci.rationale: The corpus has the forward model (Hashimoto 2022 agent-based, source: an-agent-based-model-reveals-lost-person-behavior-based-on-data-from-wilderness; Lin & Goodrich 2010 Bayesian WiSAR, source: a-bayesian-approach-to-modeling-lost-person-behaviors-based-on-terrain-features) but explicitly lacks the sighting update layer. The Hashimoto analysis states: 'No sighting/update mechanism in this paper. The model generates a prior spatial probability distribution from IPP but provides no framework for updating that distribution when a sighting (confirmed or unconfirmed) is received.' The width corpus covers the crowd-displacement danger (contradiction cluster: sighting-response-crowd-vs-privacy), but does not resolve how to mathematically update the position distribution from a valid sighting. This is SQ12-SQ14 from the prompt-decomposition. Nona's sighting integration module needs: (a) the Bayesian posterior update equation for a position distribution from a sighting at (x,y,t), (b) a reliability weighting scheme for different sighting types (trail camera > cellphone photo > verbal description), (c) how movement uncertainty grows between sighting time T and query time T+Δ based on breed/phase behavioral profile.
suggested_searches: SARBayes Bayesian sighting update lost person spatial distribution
Lin Goodrich 2010 Bayesian WiSAR posterior update sighting observation
lost dog sighting reliability eyewitness camera photo comparison
Bayesian position update wildlife tracking observation uncertainty
search and rescue sighting integration probability distribution update formula

loci.name: active-search-vs-passive-monitoring-conditioned-on-temperament
loci.one_line: For which temperament/phase combinations does active searching increase vs. decrease recovery probability — and does the corpus evidence support a threshold rule for Nona's recommendation engine?
loci.rationale: The contradiction cluster active-search-vs-passive-monitoring is high-relevance and both sides have peer-reviewed backing. Lord 2007 (lost-pet-statistics-lost-pet-research-and-recovery) shows 49% of dogs recovered by neighborhood search (highest single recovery method, Weiss 2012); MAR/Lost Dogs of America (never-follow-chase-or-pressure-a-lost-dog-lost-dogs-of-america, stay-out-of-the-woods-missing-animal-response-network) document that group searching pushes panicked dogs into traffic (top 3 death causes: car, train, drowning). The fight is genuine but both sides are right for different subpopulations. The corpus gestures at the resolution (temperament + phase determines which protocol applies) but does not provide a calibrated threshold rule. Nona's recommendation engine needs a decision boundary: at what temperament score and at what elapsed time should the recommendation switch from 'active neighborhood search' to 'passive food station + trail camera'? The depth-investigator should find whether any source has quantified this threshold or whether it remains purely practitioner heuristic.

opposing_positions.position: Active searching (neighborhood canvassing, sign posting, shelter visits, calling dog's name) significantly improves recovery probability. Lord 2007 (n=187): owners more likely to recover dogs when they called animal agencies, visited, and posted signs. Weiss 2012 (n=1,015): 49% of dog recoveries attributed to neighborhood search — the single highest recovery method. Signs become particularly effective after day 7.

Active group searching triggers flight response in panicked dogs, displaces them from safe areas, and causes vehicle strikes. Lost Dogs of America documents car/train/drowning as top 3 death causes following search-party activity. Calling the dog's name becomes an adrenaline-conditioned flight cue (MAR named cases: Lucy 10-264, Lacey 10-267, KoKo). Stay Out of the Woods principle: even solo approach to a hiding area triggers fight-or-flight in a settled survival-mode dog. Active searching is contraindicated for xenophobic temperament and for any dog post 24-48h that has entered evasion mode.

suggested_searches: Lord 2007 lost dog active search recovery method empirical
lost dog search strategy temperament passive active recovery evidence
Weiss 2012 ASPCA lost dog recovery method neighborhood search statistics
lost dog evasion mode trigger active search counterproductive evidence
xenophobic dog passive monitoring food station trail camera effectiveness

loci.name: podenco-movement-pattern-scent-vs-sight-hunting
loci.one_line: When a podenco runs lost in Algarve scrubland, does it move on scent trails (extended meandering, nose-down) or in sight-triggered bursts (directional sprint to visual contact point), and what search radius and terrain preference does each pattern imply?
loci.rationale: The corpus documents podenco as a triple-sensory hunter (sight + scent + hearing, source: prey-drive-in-podencos, about-podenco-dogs-hope-for-podencos) with Consume=8% (food lure unreliable mid-chase). But it does not resolve whether the dominant lost-dog movement pattern is scent-trail-following (which would produce a meandering trajectory following rabbit runs, staying close to established trails) or sight-triggered sprint (which would produce directional displacement to last-visual-contact point, similar to greyhound OR=8.34). This distinction changes the search area shape from a circle (random walk from IPP) to a directed lobe (prey-triggered directional displacement). The Algarve has dense rabbit warrens and maquis scrub — the terrain is maximally ambiguous for this question. The corpus gestures at this via the predatory motor sequence sources (arousal-and-predatory-motor-patterns) but does not produce a movement model. Coverage-gaps.md notes podenco-specific lost behavior as thin. This is load-bearing for Nona's search-radius and terrain-priority output for podenco cases.
suggested_searches: podenco hunting movement pattern GPS tracking scent vs sight
scent hound vs sighthound movement pattern lost dog displacement
beagle hound wandering scent trail lost dog radius
podenco Ibiza Canario hunting behavior field study distance
McLennan predatory motor sequence scent hound sight hound movement lost

skip_loci.reason: Contradiction cluster exists (Lord 2007 day-7 threshold vs. immediate posting) but decision-relevance is low — this is a secondary operational recommendation and the corpus already resolves it adequately. The resolution (post signs for sighting-call generation immediately; do not expect direct recovery before day 7) is already clear from existing sources. Depth investigation would not change Nona's core model.

Medium-relevance contradiction cluster. Both sides are right for their arousal states (lure works when settled, fails mid-chase). The corpus already resolves this via the consensus claim on olfactory shutdown + McLennan arousal data. Depth investigation would add color but not change the encoding: food_lure_effectiveness ~ f(arousal_state) is already established.

Medium-relevance contradiction cluster. Resolved adequately by the existing corpus: temperament predicts PROBABILITY of onset, not timing. The Albrecht 3-category system handles this as a prior. Further depth would be repetitive of the existing Albrecht/Buddha Dog sources and would not yield a new encodable parameter.

The sighting-response-crowd-vs-privacy contradiction cluster is well-resolved by the corpus (Side B wins on evidence quality — Winnie 7-mile/1-hour case, Princess fatal vehicle strike). The operational rule is clear: single coordinator only, never broadcast sighting location publicly. Depth investigation would confirm rather than change this rule. Covered under the bayesian-sighting-update locus instead.
Kremer 2021 (1.82x RTO multiplier, n=13,794) is already the definitive quantitative anchor. The mechanism (identification enables reunion) and the number are established. No depth needed.
